item.title: Tenkai Daily — March 15, 2026
item.description:
Open Source Releases

  • quiver-vector-db 0.1.3 — A fast, embedded vector database with Python bindings. If you’re building a local RAG pipeline and don’t want the overhead of a separate server, this is your new best friend.
  • crowdsentinel-mcp-server 0.3.3 — An MCP server for threat hunting in Elasticsearch/OpenSearch, packed with 79 tools and thousands of detection rules. Because manually sifting through logs is a special kind of hell.
  • airvo 0.1.0 — A local AI coding copilot that works with any model, no cloud needed. For when your company’s data policy is written by lawyers who’ve never heard of the internet.
  • craft-auth 4.2.1 — Cryptographic relay auth for AI agents. A serious attempt at solving the “how do we know it’s really that agent talking to our service?” problem.
  • langchain-insumer 0.10.3 — LangChain tools for blockchain interactions across 32 chains. Yes, your AI agent can now have a wallet. What could possibly go wrong?
  • deepbach-pytorch 0.1.1 — PyTorch port of the DeepBach model for automatic music harmonization. A solid, reproducible baseline if you’re into AI-generated Bach chorales.

Research Worth Reading

  • Blood Donation Llama-3.2-3B GGUF Fine-Tuned Model — A fine-tuned Llama-3.2-3B for bilingual (Swahili/English) blood donation info, in GGUF format. A neat example of efficient domain adaptation for a low-resource language, ready to run on your laptop.
  • Qwen3-0.6B Full Fine-Tuning with Unsloth and TRL — Full fine-tuning of Qwen3-0.6B using Unsloth and TRL. A handy reference implementation for when you need to train a small model properly.
  • ULS-MultiClinNERes Qwen2.5-7B LoRA for Clinical NER — A LoRA adapter on Qwen2.5-7B for clinical named entity recognition (symptoms). Shows how to adapt a big model to a niche medical NLP task without retraining the whole thing.
  • Qwen2.5-7B Fine-Tuned for OpenSCAD Code Generation — A Qwen2.5-7B specialized for generating OpenSCAD 3D modeling code. Finally, an AI that speaks the language of scriptable CAD.

Community Finds

  • Blood Donation Llama-3.2-3B LoRA Adapter — The LoRA adapter version of the above model, for parameter-efficient fine-tuning. Because sometimes you don’t need to retrain the whole universe.
  • mT5-Base PEFT Adapter for Arabic — A PEFT adapter on mT5-base for Arabic tasks. A practical example of efficient multilingual adaptation.
  • Tesslate.OmniCoder-9B-GGUF Release — The OmniCoder-9B model in GGUF format, optimized for use with llama.cpp. For when you want that model’s performance without the memory footprint.
  • Colar Qwen2.5-7B Fine-Tuned with Latent Reasoning — A Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct fine-tuned with latent reasoning techniques. The use case is “flawed fiction analysis,” which is oddly specific and kind of great.
  • Qwen3.5-40B MLX 4-Bit Quantized Model — A 4-bit MLX quantization of a Qwen3.5-40B model for Apple Silicon. For running a beefy reasoning model locally on your MacBook.
  • Aamil-0.8B 4-Bit Quantized Model with MLX — A tiny 4-bit MLX-quantized model for text generation. Proof that you can get useful conversational AI running on a potato (an Apple Silicon potato, but still).

Today’s Synthesis

The real pattern today is privacy-first, domain-specific AI that actually runs where you need it. Take the Blood Donation Llama GGUF model: it’s a fine-tuned, quantized specialist. Pair it with quiver-vector-db as an embedded vector store, and you’ve got a fully offline, bilingual medical RAG system that never leaks a byte of patient data. You could build and debug the whole pipeline using airvo as your local copilot, keeping your entire development cycle—code, data, and models—on-premise. This stack isn’t theoretical; it’s a direct response to the very real constraints of healthcare, finance, or any field where “just use the cloud” isn’t an option.

item.title: Tenkai Daily — March 14, 2026
item.description:
Open Source Releases

  • BitNet — The official inference framework for 1-bit LLMs. It gives you a way to actually run these hyper-compressed models, slashing memory and compute needs for inference.
  • openrag — A single-deploy RAG stack built on Langflow and OpenSearch. It handles the integration glue so you don’t have to, letting you get a production pipeline up faster.
  • vector-inspector 0.6.0 — A desktop GUI for poking at your vector databases. Useful for figuring out why your embeddings are weird or why search is slow.
  • OpenViking — A context database for AI agents that thinks in files and folders. It’s a structured way to manage an agent’s memory and skills for long-running tasks.
  • heretic — A tool to automatically strip the built-in censorship from LLM outputs. For when the default safety filters are getting in the way of your specific use case.
  • fish-speech — An open-source text-to-speech system gunning for SOTA quality. A solid base if you want to build voice features without depending on a proprietary API.
  • hindsight — An agent memory system designed to learn from past chats. It tackles the problem of making agents actually remember things over time.
  • cognee — A lightweight knowledge engine for agent memory that’s easy to bolt on. Cuts down the boilerplate for adding persistent memory to your agent workflows.
  • browser-use — A tool to make websites programmatically accessible for AI agents. Lets your agent click buttons and read pages, bridging the gap to the live web.

Research Worth Reading

  • Graph Tokenization for Bridging Graphs and Transformers — A method to turn graph structures into token sequences that Transformers can eat. This lets you use powerful pretrained models on graph tasks like molecular analysis.
  • Scaling Reasoning Efficiently via Relaxed On-Policy Distillation — Tweaks on-policy distillation to make training more stable. The goal is better transferring complex reasoning from a big teacher model to a smaller, deployable student.
  • Coconut by Meta AI – Better LLM Reasoning with Chain of Continuous Thought — Replaces discrete “chain-of-thought” tokens with a continuous latent representation for reasoning. The model can “think” in a more fluid, internal way before spitting out an answer.
  • Structure-Aware Epistemic Uncertainty Quantification for Neural Operator PDE Surrogates — A way to measure uncertainty in neural operators that approximate PDE solutions. Critical for trusting these models in scientific computing where confidence matters as much as the prediction.

Community Finds

  • Benchmarks and comparison of LLM AI models and API hosting providers — Artificial Analysis provides standardized, apples-to-apples benchmarks for LLM speed, cost, and performance. It’s actual data for choosing a model or provider, not just marketing claims.
  • pentatonic-agent-events 0.2.0b1 — An observability SDK for LLM apps that tracks token usage, tool calls, and chat histories. It’s the kind of monitoring you need to not fly blind in production.

Today’s Synthesis

Here’s a no-nonsense stack for building an observable, cost-efficient RAG system. Start with BitNet to run a heavily compressed model, keeping your inference costs and latency low. Plug that into openrag to get a RAG pipeline running without the usual integration headache. Once it’s live, instrument it with pentatonic-agent-events to track token burn and tool usage. Finally, use vector-inspector to visually debug your vector DB when retrieval quality inevitably gets weird. This combo covers you from model efficiency through pipeline orchestration to operational debugging.
